gold_final_answer: (a, b, c) with a | b and b | c
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: Planes through the points with integer coordinates in the three dimensional Euclidean space partition the space into unit cubes. Find all triples $(a, b, c)$, $a \le b \le c$, of positive integers such that the cubes can be colored in $abc$ colors in such a way that every parallelepiped of dimensions $a \times b \times c$, integer vertices and faces parallel to the coordinate planes does not contain cubes of the same color.
response_text:
To find all triples $(a, b, c)$ with $a \le b \le c$ such that the unit cubes in $\mathbb{Z}^3$ can be colored with $abc$ colors where every $a \times b \times c$ parallelepiped (in any of the 6 orientations parallel to the axes) has all distinct colors, we can analyze the conditions for periodic colorings.

For any 2D grid, it is a known result (often proven using polynomials and roots of unity or group algebra) that we can color the grid with $ab$ colors such that every $a \times b$ and $b \times a$ rectangle has distinct colors if and only if $a$ divides $b$ (assuming $a \le b$). 

This logic extends to 3 dimensions. We require that any box of dimensions $p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3$, where $(p_1, p_2, p_3)$ is a permutation of $(a, b, c)$, contains exactly one cube of each of the $abc$ colors. 
If we project this requirement down to the conditions of the dimensions, the existence of such a coloring implies that the dimensions must divide each other in increasing order. Thus, we must have $a$ divides $b$, and $b$ divides $c$. 

When $a \mid b$ and $b \mid c$, we can explicitly construct such a coloring by writing the coordinates in mixed radix bases (modulo $a$, modulo $b/a$, and modulo $c/b$) and assigning weights to each coordinate such that the sum modulo $abc$ uniquely identifies the color for any valid orientation of the $a \times b \times c$ parallelepiped.

Final answer: a \mid b \text{ and } b \mid c
